docs.title: Reinforcement Learning for Automated Discrete Choice Model Specification
docs.text:
Delphos is a multitask reinforcement learning agent designed to assist the discrete choice model specification process. The framework formulates model specification as a sequential decision-making problem in which Delphos progressively proposes utility specifications and receives feedback from an estimation environment through metrics such as Log-Likelihood, AIC, BIC, and convergence diagnostics.

Delphos is designed to support both methodological research in reinforcement learning for choice modelling and the practical application of trained agents for assisted model specification.

docs.title: 📈 Navigating the Specification Pareto Front
docs.text: (Above: An example of the Pareto front of specifications generated by Delphos, balancing Goodness-of-Fit and Complexity. You can also explore the Interactive Version)

docs.title: delphos-core
docs.text:
Main research and development repository containing the complete reinforcement learning framework required to train, evaluate, and extend Delphos.

Includes:

  • Markov Decision Process formulation
  • State and action spaces
  • Reward functions
  • Task manager
  • Reinforcement learning agents
  • Environment and Apollo integration
  • Training pipelines
  • Inference modules
  • Benchmarking utilities
  • Experiment configurations

docs.title: delphos (User Library)
docs.text:
Lightweight Python package for applying trained Delphos agents in inference mode to unseen or similar discrete choice modelling contexts without requiring further training.

Includes:

  • Loading pre-trained weights
  • Loading dataset schemas
  • Utility specification generation
  • Pareto-front generation
  • Inference workflows
  • Reproducible tutorials
  • Deployment examples

docs.title: delphos-datasets
docs.text:
Repository of standardized transport choice datasets used for training, inference, benchmarking, and reproducibility.

Includes:

  • Raw and processed datasets
  • Metadata and schemas
  • Variable dictionaries
  • Benchmark tasks
  • Dataset preprocessing pipelines
  • Reproducibility resources

docs.title: Reinforcement Learning for Model Specification
docs.text: Delphos formulates utility specification as a sequential decision-making problem in which an agent progressively builds and evaluates utility specifications. Moreover, Delphos can propose utility specifications based on modelling strategies learned from previous datasets and experiments.

docs.title: Multi-Task Learning
docs.text: Delphos supports training across multiple transport choice datasets to learn transferable modelling strategies rather than dataset-specific heuristics.

docs.title: Reward Functions
docs.text:
Reward functions may incorporate:

  • goodness-of-fit,
  • model complexity,
  • convergence quality,
  • parameter significance,
  • and behavioural plausibility constraints.

docs.title: Apollo Integration
docs.text: The framework integrates with the Apollo R package for estimation and evaluation of generated specifications.

docs.title: Reproducibility and Benchmarking
docs.text: The ecosystem includes standardized datasets, benchmark tasks, and reproducible workflows for evaluating reinforcement learning approaches for discrete choice model specification.

docs.title: Repository Structure
docs.text:
Multitask-Delphos/
│
├── docs/
├── tutorials/
├── examples/
├── papers/
│
├── submodules/
│   ├── delphos-core/
│   ├── delphos-user/
│   └── transport-choice-datasets/
│
├── mkdocs.yml
├── README.md
└── CONTRIBUTING.md

docs.title: Citation
docs.text: If you use Delphos, please cite the corresponding repositories and associated publications.

docs.title: Publications
docs.text:
@techreport{nova2025delphos,
  title={Delphos: A reinforcement learning framework for assisting discrete choice model specification},
  author={Nova, Gabriel and Hess, Stephane and van Cranenburgh, Sander},

  institution={TU Delft},

}

  note={Working paper}
}

docs.title: Dataset Format & Schema
docs.text:
For Delphos to understand the structure of a dataset and what variables it can use to build utility functions, it requires a Dataset Schema.

This schema is defined in JSON format.

docs.title: Schema Structure
docs.text:
A valid schema must define:

  1. alternatives: The names of the alternatives in the choice set.
  2. choice_var: The column name representing the chosen alternative.
  3. availabilities: A mapping from alternative names to their availability columns.
  4. variables: A list of attributes, their types (e.g., continuous, categorical), and which alternatives they apply to.

docs.title: Example JSON Schema
docs.text:
{
  "name": "simple_mode_choice",
  "choice_var": "choice",
  "alternatives": ["car", "bus"],
  "availabilities": {
    "car": "av_car",
    "bus": "av_bus"
  },
  "variables": [
    {
      "name": "time",
      "type": "continuous",
      "applies_to": ["car", "bus"],
      "columns": {"car": "time_car", "bus": "time_bus"}
    },
    {
      "name": "cost",
      "type": "continuous",
      "applies_to": ["car", "bus"],
      "columns": {"car": "cost_car", "bus": "cost_bus"}
    },
    {
      "name": "income",
      "type": "continuous",
      "applies_to": ["all"],
      "columns": {"all": "income"}
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Adding a Custom Dataset
docs.text:
You can easily use Delphos with your own datasets. You do not need to add them to delphos-datasets unless you want to contribute them to the project.

To use a custom dataset:

  1. Prepare your data as a CSV file.
  2. Write a JSON schema following the format described in Dataset Format.
  3. Load them into Delphos.
import pandas as pd
from delphos import DatasetSchema, DelphosAgent

# Load your custom data
my_data = pd.read_csv("path/to/my_data.csv")

# Load your custom schema
my_schema = DatasetSchema.from_json("path/to/my_schema.json")

# Run inference
agent = DelphosAgent.load_pretrained("multitask_base")
pareto = agent.explore(my_data, my_schema)

docs.title: Datasets Overview
docs.text:
The Delphos ecosystem includes a dedicated repository for datasets: delphos-datasets (hosted at transport-choice-datasets).

This repository contains a standardized collection of transportation choice datasets used for training, inference, benchmarking, and reproducibility of the Delphos framework.

By standardizing these datasets, we ensure that the multi-task reinforcement learning agent can learn robustly across different choice contexts.

docs.title: Contents
docs.text:
• Raw and processed datasets (CSV format)
  • Dataset Schemas (JSON format)
  • Variable dictionaries
  • Preprocessing pipelines

docs.title: Dataset Usage
docs.text:
To use the datasets in delphos-datasets with the delphos Python package, you can use the built-in dataset loaders.

These loaders automatically fetch the dataset and its corresponding schema, returning them in a format ready for the Delphos agent.

docs.title: Available Loaders
docs.text:
from delphos.datasets import (
    load_swissmetro,
    load_apollo_modechoice,
    load_optima
)

# Example: Loading Swissmetro
dataset_df, schema = load_swissmetro()

print(dataset_df.head())
print(schema.get_variables())

These loaders ensure that the data types and column names match exactly what the agent expects based on the schema.

docs.title: Branching Strategy
docs.text: The Delphos repositories follow a standardized branching strategy to ensure stable releases while allowing active research and development.

docs.title: Main Branches
docs.text:
• main: The primary, stable branch. Contains code that has been tested and is considered stable. This branch corresponds to the current release versions.
  • develop: The integration branch for new features and research experiments. All feature branches merge into develop before eventually being merged into main for a release.

docs.title: Supporting Branches
docs.text:
• feature/<feature-name>: Used for developing new features or conducting new experiments. Branched from develop and merged back into develop.
  • bugfix/<bug-name>: Used to fix non-critical bugs found in develop.
  • hotfix/<bug-name>: Used to quickly patch critical issues in main. Merged into both main and develop.
  • docs/<doc-updates>: For updates that strictly modify documentation.

docs.title: Example Workflow
docs.text:
# Create a new feature branch from develop
git checkout develop
git pull origin develop
git checkout -b feature/new-reward-function

# ... Make changes and commit ...

# Push and create a Pull Request to develop
git push origin feature/new-reward-function

docs.title: Contribution Workflow
docs.text: We welcome contributions to Delphos! Whether you are adding new features, fixing bugs, or improving documentation, please follow this workflow.

docs.title: 1. Open an Issue
docs.text: Before writing any code, check the issue tracker to see if your idea is already being worked on. If not, open a new issue describing the bug you found or the feature you want to add.

docs.title: 2. Setup your Development Environment
docs.text: Clone the repository and its submodules. Create a virtual environment and install the development requirements.

docs.title: 3. Create a Branch
docs.text:
Following our Branching Strategy, create a new branch for your work. 

git checkout -b feature/my-awesome-contribution develop

docs.title: 4. Make Changes
docs.text: Write your code, ensuring you follow existing style conventions. Write tests for your new features.

docs.title: 5. Commit and Push
docs.text:
Commit your changes with clear, descriptive commit messages. 

git commit -m "Add new reward function for convergence speed"
git push origin feature/my-awesome-contribution

docs.title: 6. Open a Pull Request
docs.text: Open a Pull Request (PR) against the develop branch. Link the PR to the issue you opened in Step 1. A maintainer will review your code.

docs.title: Managing Submodules
docs.text:
The Multitask-Delphos repository is the "hub" repository. The actual code lives in submodules (delphos-core, delphos (user), and delphos-datasets).

This structure allows each component to be versioned and installed independently while keeping the ecosystem logically grouped.

docs.title: Cloning the Repository
docs.text:
When cloning the main repository, you must initialize the submodules:

# Clone and initialize submodules in one command
git clone --recurse-submodules https://github.com/TUD-CityAI-Lab/Multitask-Delphos.git

If you already cloned it without submodules: 

git submodule update --init --recursive

docs.title: Updating Submodules
docs.text:
To update all submodules to the latest commits on their respective remote tracking branches:

git submodule update --remote --merge

docs.title: Making Changes to Submodules
docs.text:
If you want to contribute code to delphos-core, you must commit those changes within the submodules/delphos-core directory.

  1. cd submodules/delphos-core
  2. Make your branch, commit, and push from within that directory.
  3. Once the submodule is updated on GitHub, go back to the main repository root.
  4. Commit the new submodule pointer in the main repository: 
    cd ../.. # Back to Multitask-Delphos root
git add submodules/delphos-core
git commit -m "Update delphos-core submodule pointer"
git push

docs.title: First Example: End-to-End Walkthrough
docs.text: In this guide, we'll walk through a complete example of using Delphos to assist in specifying a discrete choice model.

docs.title: 1. Preparation
docs.text: First, ensure you have the delphos library installed and an R installation with Apollo configured. We will use one of the standard datasets from delphos-datasets (e.g., Swissmetro).

docs.title: 2. Initialize the Agent
docs.text:
We start by loading a pre-trained agent.

from delphos import DelphosAgent
from delphos.datasets import load_swissmetro

agent = DelphosAgent.load_pretrained("multitask_base")
data, schema = load_swissmetro()

docs.title: 3. Run Inference
docs.text:
We ask Delphos to explore the specification space. It will propose utility functions, estimate them using the Apollo backend, and observe the rewards (AIC/BIC improvements).

results = agent.explore(
    dataset=data,
    schema=schema,
    steps=50 # Number of exploration steps
)

docs.title: 4. Inspecting the Pareto Front
docs.text:
Delphos maintains a Pareto front of the best models found during exploration, balancing model complexity and Goodness-of-Fit.

pareto = results.get_pareto_front()
best_model = pareto.get_best_by_metric("BIC")

print(best_model.utility_equations)

docs.title: 5. Export to Apollo
docs.text:
Once you find a specification you like, you can easily export it to an Apollo R script for final reporting and analysis.

best_model.export_to_apollo("swissmetro_final.R")

You can now run Rscript swissmetro_final.R to get the full Apollo output!

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: Delphos is currently under active development. The best way to use it is by cloning the repository and installing the user library locally.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python 3.9+
  • R and the Apollo package (required for the estimation environment)

docs.title: Installing the User Library
docs.text:
To use pre-trained Delphos agents for assisted specification, you only need the delphos user library.

git clone https://github.com/TUD-CityAI-Lab/Multitask-Delphos.git
cd Multitask-Delphos/submodules/delphos-user
pip install -e .

docs.title: Installing for Development (Core)
docs.text:
If you intend to train new agents or modify the RL environment, you need to install the core repository.

git clone https://github.com/TUD-CityAI-Lab/Multitask-Delphos.git
git submodule update --init --recursive
cd Multitask-Delphos/submodules/delphos-core
pip install -r requirements.txt
pip install -e .

docs.title: Getting Started with Delphos
docs.text:
Welcome to Delphos! This section will guide you through the basics of getting up and running with the framework.

Whether you want to use a pre-trained agent to assist in specifying a discrete choice model (User Mode) or you want to train your own reinforcement learning agents (Core Mode), you'll find the necessary steps here.

docs.title: What's Next?
docs.text:
• Installation: Set up the Python environment.
  • First Example: A complete walkthrough of using Delphos.
  • Quickstart: The absolute minimum code needed to run Delphos.

docs.title: Quickstart
docs.text: If you just want to see Delphos in action as quickly as possible, here is the minimal code required to load a pre-trained agent and generate specifications for a dataset.

docs.title: The Minimal Example
docs.text:
import pandas as pd
from delphos import DelphosAgent, DatasetSchema

# 1. Load your dataset and schema
dataset = pd.read_csv("my_transport_data.csv")
schema = DatasetSchema.from_json("schema.json")

# 2. Load the pre-trained agent
agent = DelphosAgent.load_pretrained("multitask_v1")

# 3. Generate specifications
pareto_front = agent.generate_specifications(
    dataset=dataset,
    schema=schema,
    n_specs=10
)

# 4. View results
print(pareto_front.summary())

docs.title: Few-Shot Inference
docs.text:
Few-shot inference (also related to Transfer Learning) involves taking a pre-trained agent and allowing it to train for a small number of episodes on the new dataset before generating the final specifications.

This is useful when the new dataset has characteristics that are slightly different from the agent's training distribution, and you want to give the agent a chance to adapt its policy.

docs.title: Running Few-Shot Inference
docs.text:
Currently, few-shot inference is managed via the delphos-core repository, as it requires the training loop to be active.

# Pseudo-code for few-shot adaptation
from delphos_core import RLTrainer, DelphosAgent

agent = DelphosAgent.load_pretrained("multitask_base")

trainer = RLTrainer(
    agent=agent,
    dataset="my_dataset.csv",
    schema="my_dataset_schema.json"
)

# Train for a very small number of episodes (e.g., 5-10)
trainer.train(episodes=10)

# Now use the adapted agent to generate the Pareto front
pareto_front = agent.explore(steps=100)

docs.title: Training From Scratch
docs.text:
If zero-shot or few-shot inference does not yield satisfactory results, or if your dataset is highly unusual, you can train a Delphos agent entirely from scratch on your specific dataset.

This essentially treats your single dataset as the entire environment, and the agent will learn a policy specifically optimized for it.

docs.title: Running From Scratch
docs.text:
Training from scratch is done via the delphos-core repository.

from delphos_core import RLTrainer, DelphosAgent

# Initialize a new, untrained agent
agent = DelphosAgent(config="default_config.yaml")

trainer = RLTrainer(
    agent=agent,
    dataset="my_dataset.csv",
    schema="my_dataset_schema.json"
)

# Train for a larger number of episodes to learn the policy
trainer.train(episodes=100)

# Generate specifications using the fully trained agent
pareto_front = agent.explore(steps=100)

Note: Training from scratch can be computationally expensive, as every step in every episode requires estimating a discrete choice model using Apollo.

docs.title: Inference Overview
docs.text:
Delphos can be used to generate model specifications for new datasets without needing to retrain the reinforcement learning agent from scratch. This process is called Inference.

There are three primary modes of using Delphos for a new dataset:

  1. Zero-Shot Inference: Using a pre-trained agent out-of-the-box on a new dataset.
  2. Few-Shot (Transfer) Learning: Fine-tuning a pre-trained agent for a few steps on the new dataset before generating specifications.
  3. From Scratch: Training a new agent entirely on the new dataset.

The delphos user library is designed specifically to make Zero-Shot inference as easy as possible.

docs.title: Zero-Shot Inference
docs.text:
Zero-shot inference is the process of taking a Delphos agent that was trained on a set of source datasets and applying it directly to a completely new, unseen dataset.

Because Delphos learns abstract modelling strategies (e.g., "try adding a non-linear transform to continuous variables") rather than dataset-specific heuristics, it can generalize well to new data.

docs.title: Running Zero-Shot Inference
docs.text:
from delphos import DelphosAgent, DatasetSchema

# Load a generic multitask agent
agent = DelphosAgent.load_pretrained("multitask_base")

# Load your new dataset
schema = DatasetSchema.from_json("my_dataset_schema.json")

# The agent explores the new dataset without any parameter updates
pareto_front = agent.explore(
    dataset_path="my_dataset.csv",
    schema=schema,
    steps=100
)

print(pareto_front.get_best_by_metric("BIC"))

In zero-shot mode, the neural network weights of the agent are frozen. It simply uses its learned policy to navigate the specification space for the new dataset.

docs.title: Delphos Core
docs.text:
Welcome to the delphos-core repository, the heart of Multitask Delphos framework. This repository contains the complete reinforcement learning infrastructure required to:

  • formulate discrete choice model specification as a Markov Decision Process (MDP),
  • train reinforcement learning agents,
  • evaluate modelling strategies,
  • benchmark transferability across datasets,
  • and automate utility specification workflows.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text: The framework is organized into modular components.

docs.title: configs/
docs.text:
It defines the set of tasks to be used in the training process. Each discrete choice dataset can be defined as a task by creating a dataset.yaml file in dataset/ and registering it using task_registry.py.

Example:

from mdp.task import Task

TASKS: list[Task] = [
    Task.from_yaml(0, PROJECT_ROOT.parent / "dataset/dataset_1/dataset.yaml"),
    Task.from_yaml(1, PROJECT_ROOT.parent / "dataset/dataset_2/dataset.yaml"),
    Task.from_yaml(2, PROJECT_ROOT.parent / "dataset/dataset_3/dataset.yaml"),
    #...
    Task.from_yaml(N, PROJECT_ROOT.parent / "dataset/dataset_N/dataset.yaml"),
]

INFERENCE_TASKS: list[Task] = [
    Task.from_yaml(0, PROJECT_ROOT.parent / "dataset/dataset_N+1/dataset.yaml")
]

docs.title: mdp/
docs.text:
It defines defines all the core Markov Decision Process (MDP) components of Delphos. The specification process is formulated as a sequential decision-making problem in which:

  • states represent utility specifications,
  • actions represent modelling decisions,
  • and rewards evaluate the resulting estimated models.

docs.title: task.py
docs.text:
Defines the Task object, which acts as the central representation of a dataset and modelling environment. Responsible for:

  • parsing dataset.yaml files,
  • constructing attribute and covariate spaces,
  • generating structural tensors and masks,
  • and exposing environment configurations to the RL agent.

docs.title: state_manager.py
docs.text:
Defines the state space of all feasible model specification space given the attributes and covariates in the task object. It is responsible for:

  • encoding utility terms into symbolic states,
  • decoding specifications from symbolic states to utility structures,
  • generating fixed-length state representations.

docs.title: action_manager.py
docs.text:
Defines the modelling action grammar and valid actions available to the agent. It is responsible for:

  • adding utility terms,
  • modifying transformations,
  • changing taste structures,
  • adding covariate interactions,
  • and terminating specifications.

docs.title: task_runtime.py
docs.text: Encapsulates task instances, state managers, and action managers into a single environment instance.

docs.title: reward.py
docs.text:
Defines the reward functions used during training. Reward signals may incorporate:

  • AIC,
  • BIC,
  • Log-Likelihood,
  • adjusted rho-square,
  • behavioural plausibility,
  • convergence quality,
  • etc.

docs.title: env/
docs.text:
It connects Delphos with Apollo estimation software and is responsible for:

  • executing model estimations,
  • retrieving metrics,
  • caching results,
  • and coordinating communication between Python and the estimation backend.

docs.title: environment.py
docs.text:
Coordinates:

  • estimation requests,
  • reward retrieval,
  • caching,
  • and interaction between Delphos and the estimation environment.

docs.title: apollo_runner.py
docs.text:
Generates and executes Apollo estimation scripts dynamically. Responsible for:

  • Generating Apollo syntax to be ran using R,
  • launching estimation routines,
  • extracting estimation results,
  • and returning model metrics to the reinforcement learning environment.

docs.title: result_cache_sqlite.py
docs.text: Implements SQLite-based caching to avoid redundant model estimations.

docs.title: agent/
docs.text: It contains the reinforcement learning architectures and neural networks used by Delphos.

docs.title: delphos_agent.py
docs.text:
Includes:

  • policy network (a DQN),
  • modelling term embedding (a DeepSet encorder),
  • action masking,
  • exploration strategies,
  • multitask learning integration,
  • and state encoding pipelines.

docs.title: dqn.py
docs.text: Implements the Double Deep Q-Network (Double DQN) architecture used for training.

docs.title: z_state_encoder.py
docs.text: Contains permutation-invariant state encoders used to create a fixed-length state of model specifications.

docs.title: training/
docs.text:
It contains the training algorithm, where trainer.py:

Coordinates:

  • multitask training,
  • exploration,
  • replay buffer updates,
  • target network synchronization,
  • checkpointing,
  • and logging.

docs.title: transfer/
docs.text:
It contains benchmarking and evaluation workflows for unseen datasets. It supports:

  • zero-shot transfer,
  • few-shot adaptation.

docs.title: scripts/
docs.text:
Contains executable entry points for:

  • training (e.g., run_small_full_pipeline.py),
  • evaluation (e.g., run_evaluation.py),
  • benchmarking (e.g., run_transfer_benchmark.py).

Example:

python scripts/run_small_full_pipeline.py

docs.title: Reward Functions
docs.text: The reward function is the core of how Delphos learns. It translates the numerical output of an Apollo model estimation (such as Log-Likelihood or BIC) into a signal that the reinforcement learning agent can use to improve its policy.

docs.title: Default Rewards
docs.text: Delphos comes with several built-in reward functions designed for discrete choice modelling.

docs.title: BIC Reward
docs.text:
The Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC) balances Goodness-of-Fit with a penalty for the number of parameters. This is often the preferred metric for finding parsimonious models.

The reward is defined as the relative improvement in BIC over the previous best specification found by the agent.

docs.title: Log-Likelihood Reward
docs.text: A simpler reward based purely on the improvement in Log-Likelihood. This tends to encourage the agent to add more parameters until the model fails to converge.

docs.title: Defining Custom Rewards
docs.text:
You can define custom reward functions by subclassing the base RewardFunction class in delphos-core.

from mdp.reward import RewardFunction

class CustomAICReward(RewardFunction):
    def calculate(self, previous_metrics, new_metrics):
        # Your custom logic here
        aic_improvement = previous_metrics.aic - new_metrics.aic
        return max(0, aic_improvement)

docs.title: Training Delphos
docs.text: Training Delphos on a new discrete choice dataset involves three main steps.

docs.title: Step 1 — Define the dataset
docs.text:
Each dataset is configured through a dataset.yaml file.

The YAML configuration defines:

  • alternatives,
  • attributes,
  • covariates,
  • transformations,
  • baseline metrics,
  • and dataset paths.

Example:

alternatives:
  alt1:
    id: 1
    avail: "avail_1"

  alt2:
    id: 2
    avail: "avail_2"

attributes:
  time:
    id: 2
    mapping:
      alt1: "time_1"
      alt2: "time_2"

covariates:
  income:
    id: 1
    levels: [1, 2, 3]

ll_null: -1000.0
ll_linear: -800.0
n_obs: 2500

path_choice_dataset: "my_data_formatted.csv"

docs.title: Step 2 — Register the dataset as a Task object
docs.text:
Datasets are registered through the task registry.

Example:

from mdp.task import Task
from configs.task_configuration import PROJECT_ROOT

TASKS_YAML: list[Task] = [
    Task.from_yaml(
        0,
        PROJECT_ROOT.parent / "dataset/my_custom_dataset/dataset.yaml"
    ),
]

The framework automatically:

  • parses the YAML configuration,
  • generates masks,
  • builds state spaces,
  • and exposes the task to the RL environment.

docs.title: Step 3 — Run the Pipeline
docs.text:
Launch training through one of the scripts in scripts/.

Example:

python scripts/run_small_full_pipeline.py

docs.title: Training Workflow
docs.text: The training process follows three main stages.

docs.title: 1. Infrastructure Validation
docs.text:
Delphos validates:

  • dataset configurations,
  • state spaces,
  • action spaces,
  • and estimation environments.

docs.title: 2. Rollout Initialization
docs.text:
Random rollouts are executed to:

  • initialize caches,
  • validate Apollo integration,
  • and populate replay buffers.

docs.title: 3. Reinforcement Learning
docs.text: The agent progressively explores the specification space while receiving rewards from the estimation environment. Model estimations are executed dynamically using Apollo.

docs.title: Transfer Learning
docs.text:
Transfer learning is the ability of Delphos to apply knowledge gained from one set of transport choice datasets to a completely new one.

Instead of starting from a blank slate (random initialization), the agent starts with the neural network weights from a pre-trained multitask agent.

docs.title: Why Transfer Learning?
docs.text:
When a choice modeler looks at a new dataset, they don't test variables randomly. They use prior knowledge (e.g., "cost should probably be divided by income"). Delphos learns similar structural priors.

Transfer learning allows Delphos to: 1. Find good specifications much faster. 2. Avoid common specification traps (like severe multicollinearity). 3. Be useful in zero-shot or few-shot scenarios.

docs.title: Executing Transfer
docs.text:
To execute transfer learning, use the run_transfer_benchmark.py script provided in delphos-core.

python scripts/run_transfer_benchmark.py --agent pretrained_multitask.pt --target my_new_dataset.yaml

docs.title: Ready to Use: Apollo Integration
docs.text:
One of the key features of Delphos is its ability to directly output model specifications that can be run in the popular R package Apollo.

This tutorial shows how to take a specification generated by Delphos and estimate it using Apollo.

docs.title: Generating the Apollo Script
docs.text:
Once Delphos has completed its exploration phase, you can select any model from the Pareto front and export it as an Apollo R script.

# Assuming 'agent' has already explored the dataset
pareto_front = agent.get_pareto_front()

# Get the model with the best BIC score
best_model = pareto_front.get_best_by_metric("BIC")

# Export to Apollo
best_model.export_to_apollo(
    filepath="apollo_model.R",
    dataset_path="data/my_dataset.csv",
    model_name="Delphos_Best_BIC"
)

docs.title: Running the Script in R
docs.text:
The generated apollo_model.R script is fully self-contained. It includes: - Data loading. - Parameter initialization (starting values). - The utility functions defined by Delphos. - The Apollo probabilities and log-likelihood functions. - The command to run the estimation and output the results.

You can run this script directly in R or via the command line:

Rscript apollo_model.R

docs.title: Example Generated Script
docs.text:
Here is an example of what the generated R script looks like:

# --- Generated by Delphos ---
library(apollo)

# Initialize Apollo
apollo_initialise()

# Set core controls
apollo_control = list(
  modelName  = "Delphos_Best_BIC",
  modelDescr = "Auto-generated model by Delphos",
  indivID    = "ID"
)

# Load data
database = read.csv("data/my_dataset.csv")

# Define parameters
apollo_beta = c(
  asc_car   = 0,
  asc_train = 0,
  b_time    = 0,
  b_cost    = 0
)

# Define fixed parameters
apollo_fixed = c("asc_car")

# Define probabilities function
apollo_probabilities = function(apollo_beta, apollo_inputs, functionality="estimate"){
  apollo_attach(apollo_beta, apollo_inputs)
  on.exit(apollo_detach(apollo_beta, apollo_inputs))

  P = list()

  V = list()
  V[["car"]]   = asc_car + b_time * time_car + b_cost * cost_car
  V[["train"]] = asc_train + b_time * time_train + b_cost * cost_train

  mnl_settings = list(
    alternatives = c(car=1, train=2),
    avail        = list(car=avail_car, train=avail_train),
    choiceVar    = choice,
    V            = V
  )

  P[["model"]] = apollo_mnl(mnl_settings, functionality)
  P = apollo_prepareProb(P, apollo_inputs, functionality)
  return(P)
}

# Estimate model
model = apollo_estimate(apollo_beta, apollo_fixed, apollo_probabilities, apollo_inputs)

# Output results
apollo_modelOutput(model)
